decomposition_notes: Single-goal shape. All work is in the UI layer (token migration, toggle component, preference persistence, FOUC prevention). Tasks are ordered by code-coupling: tokens must land before the toggle component can reference them, and persistence must land before the FOUC script can read it. No cross-goal coordination required.

goals.title: Add a dark mode toggle to the app header
goals.why: Users running the app at night report eye strain; designers cannot share dark-mode mockups confidently because the current UI has no token system.
goals.what: Migrate hardcoded colors to daisyUI semantic tokens, add a header toggle that flips the data-theme attribute, persist the user's preference, and prevent first-paint flicker.
goals.description: v1 dark mode is a single binary toggle in the header that flips the entire interface to a low-light palette without a page reload, persisting the choice across sessions.
goals.acceptance_criteria:
Toggle in the header flips the entire app to dark mode
Preference persists across sessions
No flash of unstyled content (FOUC) on first paint
All 14 known routes render correctly in dark mode
pitfalls: Do not introduce a new theming JS library
Do not break the existing data-theme attribute pattern

tasks.title: Migrate hardcoded colors in core_components to daisyUI semantic tokens
tasks.description: Replace bg-white, text-gray-900, border-gray-200, etc. with bg-base-100, text-base-content, border-base-300 in lib/app_web/components/core_components.ex so dark mode can swap palettes by flipping data-theme alone.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
All hardcoded Tailwind colors in core_components.ex replaced with daisyUI semantic tokens
Light-mode rendering unchanged on the 14 known routes
Grep for hardcoded Tailwind color classes returns zero hits in core_components
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use daisyUI semantic tokens listed in app.css (bg-base-100, text-base-content, border-base-300, etc.)
Do not introduce hex colors or arbitrary Tailwind values
pitfalls: Do not change component prop names or behavior — color migration only

unit_tests: Render each core_components function component and assert no output contains bg-white, text-gray-*, or border-gray-* classes
Assert migrated components still emit their original semantic markup (button, input, modal wrappers) so only color classes changed
integration_tests: Render each of the 14 known routes in a LiveView test and assert the components using core_components still mount without markup errors after the token swap
manual_tests: Load a representative route in light mode and compare against the pre-migration baseline for visual parity
Flip data-theme to dark in the browser and confirm core_components elements pick up the dark palette without leftover white backgrounds
edge_cases: Components with conditional/state-based color classes (error, disabled, hover) must map every branch to a semantic token, not just the default state
Arbitrary Tailwind color values in brackets (e.g. bg-[#fff]) must also be caught, not only the named gray/white classes
testing_strategy.coverage_target: grep for hardcoded Tailwind color classes returns zero hits in core_components.ex; existing component test coverage maintained

security_considerations: No security surface: this is a pure presentational CSS class migration with no change to auth, data access, user input handling, or rendered dynamic content

tasks.title: Migrate hardcoded colors in delayed_modal to daisyUI semantic tokens
tasks.description: Same migration as the previous task, applied to lib/app_web/components/delayed_modal.ex. Necessary so modal overlays match the rest of the UI when the theme flips.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
All hardcoded Tailwind colors in delayed_modal.ex replaced with daisyUI tokens
Modal overlay backdrop uses bg-base-200/90 not bg-black/50
Light-mode modal rendering visually unchanged
tasks.patterns_to_follow: Mirror the token choices used in core_components.ex
pitfalls: Do not touch modal interaction logic — color migration only

verification_steps.step_text: Open a modal in light mode and confirm visual rendering unchanged from baseline

unit_tests: Render delayed_modal and assert the backdrop uses bg-base-200/90 rather than bg-black/50 or any hardcoded color
Assert no bg-white, text-gray-*, or border-gray-* classes remain in the rendered modal markup
integration_tests: Trigger a modal open in a LiveView test and assert the overlay and content wrappers render with the daisyUI token classes
manual_tests: Open a modal in light mode and confirm it is visually unchanged from the baseline
Open the same modal in dark mode and confirm the backdrop and panel both adopt the dark palette with adequate contrast
edge_cases: Backdrop opacity must remain visually equivalent after switching from bg-black/50 to bg-base-200/90 in both themes
Stacked or nested modals must not show a leftover light-mode backdrop layer
testing_strategy.coverage_target: grep for hardcoded Tailwind color classes returns zero hits in delayed_modal.ex; token choices match core_components.ex

security_considerations: No security surface: color-only migration of the modal component; modal interaction logic and any focus-trap behavior are untouched

tasks.title: Add user_preferences.theme column for dark mode persistence
tasks.description: Add a string column theme to user_preferences (or create the table if absent) with values 'light' or 'dark'. Default 'light'. Adds the storage that the LiveView toggle will write to and the FOUC script will read from.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Migration creates or extends user_preferences with a theme string column
Default value is 'light'
User schema exposes the new field via the existing changeset
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Mirror the existing migration style under priv/repo/migrations/
Use a string column with a check constraint, not an enum (daisyUI grows palettes; check constraint can widen)
pitfalls: Do not migrate existing rows — let them fall back to the default

unit_tests: Assert the User (or UserPreferences) changeset casts theme and rejects values other than 'light' or 'dark'
Assert the schema field defaults to 'light' when no theme is provided
Assert the check constraint surfaces as a changeset error (not a raw DB exception) on an invalid theme value
integration_tests: Insert a user_preferences row without a theme and assert it reads back as 'light'
Run the migration up then down on a clean test DB and assert the schema is added and cleanly reverted
manual_tests: Run mix ecto.migrate against the dev DB and confirm the column and check constraint exist
edge_cases: Pre-existing user_preferences rows must fall back to the default rather than being backfilled
Case sensitivity: 'Dark' or 'LIGHT' should be rejected by the check constraint, only lowercase allowed
Table-absent path: migration must create user_preferences if it does not already exist
testing_strategy.coverage_target: mix ecto.migrate runs successfully; new changeset/validation branches for the theme field covered by tests

security_considerations: Data-access scoping: the theme preference must be written and read only for the current authenticated user's own user_preferences row, never keyed by a client-supplied user id
Input validation: constrain theme to the 'light'/'dark' allow-list via changeset validation plus a DB check constraint to prevent arbitrary strings being persisted
No PII: the stored value is a low-sensitivity UI preference, not personal data

tasks.title: Add theme_toggle LiveView component and wire it into the header
tasks.description: Add a small LiveView component that renders a sun/moon icon button. Clicking it sends a phx-click event that updates the user's preference and pushes the data-theme attribute change to the DOM via a JS hook.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Component renders in the app header
Click toggles between light/dark in under 50ms
User preference column updates atomically with the visual change
Keyboard accessible (Enter/Space activate)
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Use the existing core_components button + icon idioms
Update the preference through MyApp.Accounts, not Repo directly
pitfalls: Do not flash the wrong-theme paint on toggle — push the attribute change before the LiveView re-renders

verification_steps.step_text: Click the toggle in both modes; observe no FOUC and the preference persists across reloads

unit_tests: Render the theme_toggle component and assert it emits a button with the sun/moon icon and an accessible label
Assert the button carries the phx-click event and is reachable/activatable via keyboard (Enter/Space), not click-only
integration_tests: In a LiveView test, mount the header, send the toggle event, and assert the user's theme preference is updated through the Accounts context (not Repo directly)
Assert the toggle event pushes the data-theme attribute change to the client (push_event / JS hook) so the DOM flips without a full re-render
manual_tests: Click the toggle in both light and dark mode and confirm the flip happens under ~50ms with no wrong-theme flash
Reload the page after toggling and confirm the chosen theme persists across the session
Tab to the toggle and activate it with Enter and Space to confirm keyboard accessibility
edge_cases: Rapid double-clicks must not desync the persisted preference from the visible theme
Guest (unauthenticated) users toggling before login should still flip the theme without erroring on the missing preference row
The attribute push must land before the LiveView re-render to avoid a wrong-theme paint on toggle
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Component render, keyboard activation, and preference-update event paths covered; toggle event handler branches exercised in tests

security_considerations: Data-access scoping: the toggle must update only the current authenticated user's preference via MyApp.Accounts using current_scope, never a user id taken from the client event payload
User input handling: the toggle event should switch between the known 'light'/'dark' values server-side rather than persisting an arbitrary theme string sent from the DOM
Authorization: the phx-click handler must reject preference writes from an unauthenticated socket (or scope them to the guest cookie), preventing one session from mutating another user's preference

tasks.title: Add FOUC-prevention script to read the theme preference before CSS loads
tasks.description: Add a tiny inline script at the top of the head that reads the theme preference (from cookie for guests, from data attribute on body for logged-in users injected server-side) and sets data-theme on the html element before stylesheets load. Eliminates the wrong-theme flash on first paint.
tasks.acceptance_criteria:
Inline script runs before <link rel="stylesheet"> tags
No visible wrong-theme flash on first paint in dark mode
Script is <500 bytes inline so it doesn't bloat the document head
tasks.patterns_to_follow:
Server-side renders the user's preference into a data attribute on body when authenticated
Falls back to a cookie for guests so toggle works pre-login too
pitfalls: Do not load external JS for this — it MUST be inline to beat the FOUC window

verification_steps.step_text: Hard-reload in dark mode and watch for a flash — there must be none

unit_tests: Render root.html.heex for an authenticated user and assert the body carries the server-rendered theme data attribute matching the user's stored preference
Assert the inline theme script appears in the head before any <link rel="stylesheet"> tag
integration_tests: Request a page as a guest with a theme cookie set to 'dark' and assert the inline script path reads the cookie value
Request a page as an authenticated dark-mode user and assert the rendered document seeds data-theme from the body data attribute, not the cookie
manual_tests: Hard-reload a route in dark mode and watch for a wrong-theme flash on first paint — there must be none
Clear the cookie and load as a guest to confirm the fallback path defaults cleanly without a flash
edge_cases: Missing/invalid cookie or data attribute must fall back to the default theme without throwing and blanking the page
Script must set data-theme on <html> before stylesheets load to actually beat the FOUC window
Inline script must stay under 500 bytes so it does not bloat the document head
testing_strategy.coverage_target: Inline script presence and ordering asserted in the root layout render test; authenticated (data-attribute) and guest (cookie) branches both covered

security_considerations: CSP: an inline <script> in the head requires either a nonce or an explicit allowance in the Content-Security-Policy; a strict nonce CSP will silently drop an un-nonced inline script and reintroduce the flash

XSS / output encoding: validate the theme value from the cookie or server-injected data attribute against the 'light'/'dark' allow-list before writing it into data-theme, so an attacker-controlled cookie cannot inject markup into the inline script
Cookie integrity: the guest theme cookie is a low-sensitivity, non-authentication value and must not be trusted as anything more than a UI hint — never used for access decisions
